content.text: Not all examples are equally useful in a few-shot prompt. The choice of which examples to include — and how many — has a measurable impact on output quality, often larger than adding more examples of the wrong kind. Exemplar selection is the systematic practice of choosing examples that maximally reduce the model's uncertainty about the task boundary, label distribution, and expected output format. Poor exemplar selection can even hurt performance: a set of examples that all come from one label class will bias the model toward that class, and examples that use an inconsistent format teach the model to be inconsistent.
Principles of Good Exemplar Selection
Principle
Why it matters
Common mistake
Label balance
Prevents class bias in classification tasks
Using only easy, clear-cut examples of one class
Boundary coverage
Teaches the model where one label ends and another begins
Only including unambiguous examples — easy cases teach nothing about hard cases
Format consistency
Every example uses the same delimiter pattern
Mixing "Label:" and "Answer:" between examples
Domain relevance
Examples should come from the same domain as production inputs
Using examples from a different domain than the real queries
Query last
Recent tokens carry more weight — put the test query last
Interleaving query and examples
Static vs Dynamic Exemplar Selection

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


def classify(examples: list[tuple[str, str]], query: str) -> str:
    shots = "\n\n".join(f"Text: {t}\nLabel: {l}" for t, l in examples)
    prompt = f"{shots}\n\nText: {query}\nLabel:"
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=10,
        temperature=0.0,
        stop_sequences=["\n"],
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text.strip()


# Full example pool — covers all classes and edge cases
POOL = [
    ("This is the best purchase I have made this year!",          "Positive"),
    ("Fast shipping and exactly as described.",                   "Positive"),
    ("Stopped working after one week. Terrible quality.",        "Negative"),
    ("Customer support was rude and unhelpful.",                 "Negative"),
    ("It does the job. Nothing extraordinary.",                  "Neutral"),
    ("Packaging was damaged but the product inside was fine.",   "Neutral"),  # boundary case
    ("Good build quality, but instructions are confusing.",      "Neutral"),  # mixed signals
]

QUERY = "The item looks great but arrived two weeks late."

# Strategy 1: Fixed balanced set — one example per class + boundary case
balanced = [POOL[0], POOL[2], POOL[4], POOL[5]]
print("Balanced:  ", classify(balanced, QUERY))

# Strategy 2: Skewed — only positive examples (bad practice)
skewed = POOL[:2]
print("Skewed:    ", classify(skewed, QUERY))

# Strategy 3: Dynamic — pick the example closest to the query by keyword overlap
def similarity(a: str, b: str) -> float:
    a_words, b_words = set(a.lower().split()), set(b.lower().split())
    return len(a_words & b_words) / max(len(a_words | b_words), 1)

def dynamic_select(pool: list[tuple[str, str]], query: str, n: int = 3) -> list[tuple[str, str]]:
    scored = sorted(pool, key=lambda ex: similarity(ex[0], query), reverse=True)
    # Ensure at least one example per class that appears in top candidates
    selected = scored[:n]
    labels = {l for _, l in selected}
    # Fill missing classes from pool
    for text, label in pool:
        if label not in labels and len(selected) < n + 2:
            selected.append((text, label))
            labels.add(label)
    return selected[:n]

dynamic = dynamic_select(POOL, QUERY)
print("Dynamic:   ", classify(dynamic, QUERY))
print("  (selected:", [l for _, l in dynamic], ")")
How Many Examples?

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

EXAMPLES = [
    ("Absolutely love it — exceeded every expectation.",         "Positive"),
    ("Complete waste of money. Broke immediately.",              "Negative"),
    ("Average product. Does what it says.",                      "Neutral"),
    ("The design is elegant, but delivery took three weeks.",    "Neutral"),
    ("Fantastic quality, highly recommend to everyone.",         "Positive"),
]

TEST_QUERIES = [
    "Works fine but the packaging could be better.",
    "Worst experience I've had shopping online.",
    "Exactly what I needed for a reasonable price.",
]


def classify_n(n_shots: int, query: str) -> str:
    shots = "\n\n".join(f"Text: {t}\nLabel: {l}" for t, l in EXAMPLES[:n_shots])
    prompt = f"{shots}\n\nText: {query}\nLabel:"
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=10,
        temperature=0.0,
        stop_sequences=["\n"],
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text.strip()


print(f"{'Query':<48}", "  0  1  3  5")
for q in TEST_QUERIES:
    results = [classify_n(n, q) for n in [0, 1, 3, 5]]
    labels = "  ".join(r[:3] for r in results)
    print(f"{q[:47]:<48}  {labels}")
# In practice, 3 balanced examples often match 5+.
# More examples help most on borderline inputs.

Exemplar selection is where few-shot prompting pays its biggest dividends. Three carefully chosen examples — one per class, with a boundary-case example included — will consistently outperform five randomly picked examples. The selection criteria in order of importance: label balance first, boundary case coverage second, domain match third, and format consistency throughout. Dynamic selection (choosing examples similar to the query at runtime) further improves performance on diverse input distributions but requires a retrieval step and a maintained example pool.
Why Exemplar Selection Matters

In few-shot prompting, the examples supplied in the prompt serve as the model's implicit task specification. The model has no other way to infer what "correct" looks like — it reads the pattern from the examples. Research (Brown et al., 2020; Lu et al., 2022) has shown that the choice of which examples to include can change accuracy on classification tasks by 10–40 percentage points on the same model without any weight changes. This makes exemplar selection one of the highest-leverage interventions available to a practitioner working with a frozen language model.

The pain points are concrete: a sentiment analysis prompt with three positive examples and zero negative examples will heavily bias the model toward positive labels, even on clearly negative inputs. A coding assistant prompt with examples that use camelCase variable names will generate camelCase code even when the user's codebase uses snake_case. Format inconsistency between examples — mixing "Label:" and "Answer:" — teaches the model that inconsistency is acceptable, degrading output structure. These errors are invisible when you only check a handful of outputs manually.
Architecture: The Exemplar Selection Pipeline

attrs.alt: Exemplar selection pipeline: example pool → selection strategy (static/dynamic/random) → prompt builder → LLM → output

content.text: Benefits of Principled Exemplar Selection
Reduces output variance.
Carefully chosen examples anchor the model's output format, reducing prompt sensitivity and making outputs more predictable and easier to parse downstream.
Eliminates class bias.
Balanced label representation in examples prevents the model from defaulting to the majority class, a common failure in imbalanced classification tasks.
Teaches boundary cases.
Boundary examples (inputs near decision boundaries) teach the model where one label ends and another begins — the most informative signal for classification, analogous to support vectors in SVMs.
No fine-tuning required.
Good exemplars can match or exceed the performance of fine-tuning on small labelled datasets, with no GPU time and no model serving infrastructure changes.
Dynamic selection adapts to query.
Embedding-based retrieval selects the most relevant examples for each specific query at runtime, handling diverse input distributions that a single fixed example set cannot cover.
Trade-offs and Limitations
Context window cost.

Each example consumes tokens. A 5-shot prompt with 200-token examples uses 1000 context tokens before the query — significant for long documents and expensive at scale. More examples is not always better.
Dynamic selection adds latency.

Embedding-based retrieval requires encoding the query, searching an index, and potentially re-ranking — adding 20–200 ms to each request. For low-latency applications, pre-computed static exemplar sets may be preferable.
Example pool curation is manual.
Building a high-quality, diverse pool of labelled examples requires domain expertise. Poor pool curation propagates to every query that uses dynamic selection.
Recency and primacy effects.

LLMs are not uniformly sensitive to all positions. The last few examples (recency) and the first example (primacy) tend to have outsized influence on output. Putting the best boundary example last is generally beneficial.
When to Use Each Selection Strategy

Static exemplar sets work well when the input distribution is narrow and predictable — for example, a customer service bot that always handles the same 10 types of requests. The examples can be hand-curated once and serve all queries equally well. Dynamic selection is preferable when the input distribution is broad or unpredictable, as in a general-purpose QA or code assistant. Avoid random selection except as a baseline for evaluation — it is consistently outperformed by either of the deliberate strategies.
Strategy
Latency overhead
Best for
Weakness
Static (fixed set)
None
Narrow, predictable inputs; production latency constraints
Fails on out-of-distribution queries
Dynamic (retrieval)
Diverse inputs; complex tasks with many subtypes
Requires embedding infrastructure; pool curation effort
Random
None
Baseline evaluation only
Unpredictable; can introduce label bias
Chain-of-thought (CoT)
None (static CoT)
Multi-step reasoning tasks
Examples are much longer; higher token cost
Real-World Example: Production Intent Classification

A customer support system needs to classify user messages into 8 intents (billing, cancellation, technical issue, feature request, etc.). The team has a labelled dataset of 10,000 historical tickets. Rather than fine-tuning, they implement dynamic exemplar selection: each incoming ticket is embedded using a fast sentence transformer, and the 2 most similar examples per intent class (16 examples total) are retrieved and used as the prompt. This approach reaches 89% accuracy on a held-out test set, matching the accuracy of a fine-tuned classifier but with no training time and easy updating — adding new intent examples to the pool immediately improves coverage without retraining.

import anthropic
import numpy as np
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
from collections import defaultdict

# ── Production-grade dynamic exemplar selection ────────────────────────────
client = anthropic.Anthropic()
embed_model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")

# Labelled example pool — in production this would be 100s of examples
POOL = [
    # (text, intent)
    ("I was charged twice for last month's subscription.", "billing"),
    ("Why does my invoice show a charge I don't recognise?", "billing"),
    ("I want to cancel my account immediately.", "cancellation"),
    ("How do I close my subscription?", "cancellation"),
    ("The app keeps crashing when I open the settings page.", "technical_issue"),
    ("I'm getting a 500 error when I try to export my data.", "technical_issue"),
    ("It would be great if you could add dark mode.", "feature_request"),
    ("Can you add support for CSV exports?", "feature_request"),
    ("I can't remember my password. How do I reset it?", "account_access"),
    ("My two-factor authentication code isn't working.", "account_access"),
]

# Pre-compute embeddings for the pool
pool_texts = [t for t, _ in POOL]
pool_labels = [l for _, l in POOL]
pool_vecs = embed_model.encode(pool_texts, normalize_embeddings=True)


def select_exemplars(
    query: str,
    k_per_class: int = 1,
) -> list[tuple[str, str]]:
    """Select the best exemplars for a query using per-class retrieval."""
    q_vec = embed_model.encode([query], normalize_embeddings=True)[0]
    sims = pool_vecs @ q_vec

    # Group by label, pick top-k per label
    by_label = defaultdict(list)
    for idx in np.argsort(sims)[::-1]:
        label = pool_labels[idx]
        if len(by_label[label]) < k_per_class:
            by_label[label].append((pool_texts[idx], label, float(sims[idx])))

    # Flatten and sort by similarity; put highest-similarity last (recency effect)
    selected = [(t, l) for items in by_label.values() for t, l, _ in items]
    selected.sort(key=lambda x: float(pool_vecs[pool_texts.index(x[0])] @ q_vec))
    return selected


def classify_intent(query: str) -> str:
    exemplars = select_exemplars(query)
    shots = "\n".join(f"Customer: {t}\nIntent: {l}" for t, l in exemplars)
    intents = sorted(set(pool_labels))
    system = (
        f"Classify the customer message into exactly one of these intents: "
        f"{', '.join(intents)}. Reply with only the intent name."
    )
    prompt = f"{shots}\n\nCustomer: {query}\nIntent:"
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=20,
        temperature=0.0,
        system=system,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text.strip()


# Test
test_queries = [
    "My credit card was charged $49.99 but I cancelled last week.",
    "The login page won't load on Firefox.",
    "Please add the ability to export to PDF.",
]
for q in test_queries:
    print(f"Query: {q[:60]}")
    print(f"Intent: {classify_intent(q)}\n")
Common Mistakes
Imbalanced label distribution.
Using 4 positive examples and 1 negative example in a binary classification prompt will bias the model heavily toward positive. Always use equal counts per label, or use a balanced sampling strategy.
Using only easy, clear-cut examples.
An example pool composed entirely of unambiguous cases fails to teach the model about edge cases. Explicitly include 1–2 near-boundary examples per prompt.
Inconsistent formatting between examples.
Mixing "Label:" and "Answer:" and "Classification:" as label prefixes teaches the model that the format is flexible, leading to unparseable outputs.
Domain mismatch between examples and queries.

Using movie review sentiment examples for financial news sentiment classification produces systematically worse results than using domain-matched financial examples, even if the task is ostensibly the same.
Putting the query before the examples.
Placing the test query before the examples rather than after breaks the established input→output pattern. The query must always come last.
Best Practices
Maintain a curated, versioned example pool. Treat it like labelled training data — document each example, its source, and why it was included. Pool quality directly determines prompt quality.
Always include at least one near-boundary or ambiguous example per decision boundary. These teach the model where the task is hardest.
Use 3–5 examples for most tasks. Empirically, the marginal benefit of additional examples drops sharply after 5, while token cost continues to grow linearly.
For dynamic selection, embed both examples and queries with the same model. Avoid embedding the pool with one model and the query with another — the similarity scores will be meaningless.
Evaluate your exemplar selection strategy with a proper offline evaluation: compare static vs. dynamic selection on a held-out set before deploying.
Performance and Scalability

The token cost of few-shot examples scales linearly with the number of examples and their length. At typical API pricing, 5 examples of 150 tokens each add 750 tokens to every request — multiplied by millions of requests per day, this is significant. For latency-sensitive applications, consider (1) compressing examples to their shortest informative form, (2) using a smaller, faster model for classification when exemplars are well-chosen, or (3) caching the embedding and retrieval step when the query input arrives in predictable clusters.

Dynamic selection at scale requires an efficient approximate nearest-neighbour (ANN) index. For pools up to ~10,000 examples, numpy dot-product search is fast enough (< 1 ms). For larger pools, use FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search) or a vector database (Pinecone, Weaviate, Chroma) for sub-millisecond retrieval at million-example scale.
Tooling and Ecosystem
LangChain ExampleSelector
— SemanticSimilarityExampleSelector and MaxMarginalRelevanceExampleSelector implement embedding-based and diversity-maximising selection out of the box.
sentence-transformers
— Fast, lightweight embedding models (all-MiniLM-L6-v2, all-mpnet-base-v2) for embedding query and pool texts for similarity-based retrieval.
FAISS
(Facebook AI Similarity Search) — GPU-accelerated approximate nearest-neighbour search for large example pools.
PromptFlow (Azure) / LangSmith
— Evaluation harnesses for comparing exemplar selection strategies with systematic offline metrics.
Security and Reliability

Exemplar pools that contain user-generated content must be sanitised before use. If attackers can inject text into the example pool (for example, by submitting product reviews that get added as examples), they can use prompt injection to manipulate model behaviour for other users. This is a real attack vector in systems where exemplars are drawn from production logs. Validate and sanitise all pool content, and never automatically add user-submitted content to the pool without human review.

Reliability risk: dynamic selection can introduce non-determinism — the same query can receive different examples depending on pool updates, leading to inconsistent outputs. For regulated use cases (medical, legal, financial), consider static curated exemplar sets with change-control processes, rather than dynamic retrieval that can change without notice.
Key Takeaways
Exemplar selection is the highest-leverage intervention in few-shot prompting: example choice can change accuracy by 10–40% without any model changes.
Label balance is the top priority: always use equal counts per class to prevent the model from defaulting to the majority label.
Include near-boundary examples: ambiguous, edge-case inputs teach the model the decision boundary better than clear-cut cases.
Format consistency is mandatory: all examples must use identical delimiter patterns, field names, and label formats.
3–5 examples is the practical sweet spot: marginal gains diminish after 5 while token cost grows linearly.
Dynamic (embedding-based) selection outperforms static for diverse query distributions; static is preferred for narrow, predictable inputs and low-latency requirements.
Query must always appear last in the prompt to benefit from the model's recency bias toward recent context.
Sanitise user-generated content before adding it to example pools to prevent prompt injection attacks.
